event: Stone Age hunter-gatherers inhabit the region, leaving Middle Stone Age tools across present-day Zimbabwe.
Ancestors of the San people occupy the area, later producing the region's renowned rock art in caves and shelters.
Bantu-speaking farming communities migrate into the region, introducing iron-working, cattle-keeping, and crop cultivation.
Early Iron Age communities of the Zhizo and Leopard's Kopje cultures establish settled villages on the Zimbabwe plateau.
Mapungubwe and related Shona-speaking polities develop trade in gold and ivory linked to the Indian Ocean coast.
Construction begins on the stone enclosures of Great Zimbabwe, capital of a powerful Shona trading state.
Great Zimbabwe reaches its height, controlling regional gold trade with Swahili coastal ports such as Sofala.
Nyatsimba Mutota founds the Kingdom of Mutapa (Monomotapa) in the north, dominating the Zambezi valley gold trade.
Great Zimbabwe is largely abandoned, likely due to resource exhaustion and shifting trade routes.
The Torwa state rises around Khami in the southwest, building elaborate stone-walled settlements.
Portuguese traders establish coastal posts and begin seeking access to the gold of the Mutapa kingdom.
Portuguese Jesuit missionary Gonçalo da Silveira is killed at the Mutapa court, prompting Portuguese military interest.
Mutapa ruler Mavura signs treaties making the kingdom a tributary vassal of the Portuguese crown.
The Rozvi Empire under Changamire Dombo defeats the Portuguese and expels them from the plateau interior.
Ndebele people under Mzilikazi, fleeing the Zulu Mfecane, settle in the southwest and establish the Ndebele Kingdom at Bulawayo.
Lobengula succeeds Mzilikazi as king of the Ndebele following a contested succession.
Lobengula signs the Rudd Concession, granting Cecil Rhodes's agents mining rights that the British exploit to claim the territory.
Cecil Rhodes's British South Africa Company receives a royal charter to administer and colonize the region.
The Pioneer Column of the British South Africa Company occupies Mashonaland and founds Fort Salisbury (now Harare).
The First Matabele War breaks out, ending with the company's defeat of the Ndebele and the death of King Lobengula.
The territory is named Rhodesia after Cecil Rhodes.
The Ndebele and Shona launch the First Chimurenga uprising against company rule, brutally suppressed by 1897.
Southern Rhodesia becomes a self-governing British colony after white settlers vote against joining South Africa.
The Land Apportionment Act entrenches racial segregation by reserving the best farmland for white settlers.
Southern Rhodesia joins the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland alongside Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland.
The Federation dissolves, and the Zimbabwe African National Union (ZANU) splits from ZAPU as nationalist movements grow.
Prime Minister Ian Smith issues a Unilateral Declaration of Independence (UDI) to preserve white minority rule.
The Second Chimurenga liberation war begins as guerrilla forces clash with Rhodesian security forces.
Rhodesia formally declares itself a republic, severing remaining ties to the British crown.
Intensified guerrilla warfare by ZANLA and ZIPRA escalates the armed struggle against the Smith regime.
A short-lived internal settlement creates Zimbabwe-Rhodesia, while the Lancaster House Agreement charts a path to majority rule.
Zimbabwe gains internationally recognized independence on 18 April, with Robert Mugabe as prime minister, and joins the UN.
The Gukurahundi massacres begin in Matabeleland as the Fifth Brigade kills thousands of Ndebele civilians.
The Unity Accord merges ZANU and ZAPU, and Mugabe becomes executive president under a revised constitution.
Zimbabwe adopts a structural adjustment program (ESAP) amid economic pressure from international lenders.
Large unbudgeted payouts to war veterans and currency turmoil trigger the start of severe economic decline.
Zimbabwe sends troops to intervene in the Second Congo War, straining the national economy.
The government launches the fast-track land reform program, seizing white-owned commercial farms and collapsing agricultural output.
Zimbabwe is suspended from the Commonwealth amid disputed presidential elections and the country later withdraws.
Hyperinflation peaks at astronomical levels, forcing abandonment of the Zimbabwe dollar after a violently disputed election.
A power-sharing government forms with Mugabe as president and Morgan Tsvangirai as prime minister, and foreign currencies are adopted.
A new constitution is approved by referendum and Mugabe wins a contested presidential election.
A military intervention forces Robert Mugabe to resign after 37 years in power, and Emmerson Mnangagwa becomes president.
Zimbabwe reintroduces a national currency, but economic crisis and inflation persist.
Emmerson Mnangagwa wins re-election in polls criticized by observers as falling short of democratic standards.
Zimbabwe introduces a new gold-backed currency, the ZiG, to combat persistent inflation and currency instability.
